skill.name: file-search
skill.description: Use fd, ripgrep, and fzf to find files, search content, preview matches, and build efficient codebase search workflows.
skill.summary: Guides fast file discovery, content search, and fuzzy selection with fd, ripgrep, and fzf.
skill.author: 0xDarkMatter

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many command patterns because this skill is Bash-focused documentation for fd, ripgrep, and fzf. The weak-crypto and dynamic-import alerts are false positives, but the skill does include legitimate examples that can modify or delete files when executed. No evidence found for malware, credential access, network exfiltration, obfuscation, or prompt injection.

medium_findings.title: Filesystem-changing Bash examples
medium_findings.description: The advanced workflow guide includes examples that delete selected files, rename files, format files in place, and write generated output. These are legitimate search workflows, but an agent could cause data loss if it runs them without confirmation.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible documentation examples and include explicit delete, rename, and write operations. They are not malicious by intent, but the data-loss risk is concrete if executed carelessly.

medium_findings.title: Command execution through fd, fzf, xargs, and shell functions
medium_findings.description: The skill teaches pipelines that pass selected files into editors, git commands, preview commands, and shell functions. This is expected for the skill, but it creates command-execution risk when selections or file names are untrusted.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The static command findings are true as command patterns, and the surrounding text confirms they are intentional Bash workflows. Legitimate use is clear, so the issue is moderate rather than high.

low_findings.title: False positive weak cryptography alerts
low_findings.description: The high-severity weak-cryptography detections point to descriptive and heading text, not cryptographic code. No hash algorithm, encryption routine, or security-sensitive cryptographic use appears at these locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line-numbered review shows ordinary skill metadata and an output-mode heading. There is no evidence of MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or other weak cryptography use at the flagged lines.

low_findings.title: False positive dynamic import alert
low_findings.description: The dynamic import alert points to a ripgrep command that searches Python import statements and writes a list to imports.txt. It is not JavaScript import() execution and does not load code dynamically.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged context is a shell example for dead-code analysis. The syntax is text search and command substitution, not a runtime dynamic import operation.

dangerous_patterns.title: Destructive xargs examples
dangerous_patterns.description: Several examples pipe fuzzy-selected files to rm or other commands. Confirmation flags reduce risk in one case, but the pattern can still delete files.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands explicitly invoke rm through xargs. The examples are intentional workflows, but they are dangerous if applied to the wrong selection.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell substitution and generated file lists
dangerous_patterns.description: The project-analysis examples use command substitution and generated file lists. These are common shell patterns, but they can behave unexpectedly with unusual file names.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The shell patterns are real and visible, but they are presented as local workflow examples. Risk depends on user input and file-name handling rather than malicious intent.

content.user_title: Search Files Faster with fd, rg, and fzf
content.value_statement: Large codebases make file discovery and text search slow. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code to use fd, ripgrep, and fzf for precise search workflows.
actual_capabilities: Find files by name, extension, type, size, and modification time using fd.
Search file contents with ripgrep using case, context, type, and multiline options.
Use fzf to select files interactively and preview matches before opening them.
Combine fd, rg, fzf, awk, xargs, and git for code investigation workflows.
Create shell functions for repeated file search, edit, and git staging tasks.
Tune search performance with ignore files, fixed-string searches, and path narrowing.
limitations: Requires fd and ripgrep to be installed before commands will work.
fzf workflows need an interactive terminal and may not work in every agent environment.
Some examples can modify or delete files if run without review.
The skill provides command guidance, not a standalone search application.

use_cases.title: Navigate a large repository
use_cases.description: Find source files, tests, and configuration files quickly without scanning the whole tree manually.

use_cases.title: Investigate code changes
use_cases.description: Search for patterns, preview matching lines, and open the most relevant files from the terminal.

use_cases.title: Build repeatable search shortcuts
use_cases.description: Create shell functions that combine file discovery, fuzzy selection, previews, and editor launch commands.

prompt_templates.title: Find a file type
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the file-search skill to find all TypeScript files under src and explain the fd command you would run.

prompt_templates.title: Search code text
prompt_templates.prompt: Use ripgrep patterns from this skill to search for TODO or FIXME comments and summarize the files with the most matches.

prompt_templates.title: Preview before editing
prompt_templates.prompt: Use fd, rg, and fzf to design a command that previews matching files before opening one in my editor.

prompt_templates.title: Create an advanced workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a safe shell workflow that finds deprecated API usage, previews each match, and asks before making any file changes.

output_examples.input: Find Python files changed today and search for async functions.
output_examples.output: The assistant suggests narrowing with fd by extension and modified time, then running ripgrep for async definitions inside those files.

output_examples.input: Open a matching search result from an interactive list.
output_examples.output: The assistant proposes a pipeline that sends ripgrep results into fzf, previews nearby context, and opens the selected file at the matching line.

output_examples.input: Clean up files matching a pattern safely.
output_examples.output: The assistant recommends previewing the selected files first and requiring explicit confirmation before any delete or rewrite command runs.

best_practices: Preview search results before opening, editing, deleting, or staging files.
Start with narrow paths, file types, and fixed-string searches when possible.
Ask for confirmation before running workflows that write, move, or remove files.
anti_patterns: Do not pipe broad fuzzy selections directly into rm without confirmation.
Do not run formatting or rewrite commands across a repository before reviewing the match set.
Do not assume fzf workflows work in noninteractive shells or restricted agent sessions.

faq.question: What tools does this skill use?
faq.answer: It focuses on fd for file discovery, ripgrep for content search, and fzf for interactive selection.

faq.question: Can it search inside code files?
faq.answer: Yes. It provides ripgrep patterns for matching text, filtering by file type, and showing context lines.

faq.question: Does it require installation steps?
faq.answer: Yes. fd and ripgrep are required, and fzf is needed for interactive workflows.

faq.question: Can it modify files?
faq.answer: The skill includes examples that can format, rename, write, or delete files. Review commands before running them.

faq.question: Is this only for Claude?
faq.answer: No. The marketplace metadata lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: What is the safest way to use it?
faq.answer: Use read-only searches first, preview selections, and require confirmation before any command changes files.
